ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694) but no specific entry for nasi goreng with beef and black pepper. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category. No specific code exists for nasi goreng beef black pepper variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for nasi goreng beef black pepper. SNOMED CT does not have granular codes for this dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of nasi goreng beef black pepper is approximately 350 g, typically presented as a single plate with fried rice, sliced or minced beef, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice with Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for nasi goreng (fried rice with egg). Values have been adapted upward for protein and fat to account for the addition of beef and black pepper seasoning, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked beef (lean, pan-fried). The base carbohydrate and energy profile follows the HPB nasi goreng entry. IMR Malaysia MyFCD nasi goreng entries were cross-referenced for validation. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, fried (nasi goreng analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi goreng beef black pepper. GI of 54 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice / nasi goreng (white rice base, stir-fried), which reports GI values in the range 50–56 (low). The addition of beef and black pepper does not materially alter the glycemic response of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 77.0) / 100 ≈ 41.6, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 22.0 × 3.5 = 77.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving) from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid nasi goreng beef black pepper due to its very high sodium content (~1680 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents approximately 73% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or smaller portions is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 54, at the upper end of the low GI category) and high glycemic load per serving (~41.6) from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with nasi goreng beef black pepper because the white rice base produces a low-to-medium glycemic response (GI ~54) and the large serving size results in a high glycemic load (~41.6), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control to half a plate and pairing with non-starchy vegetables is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) from cooking oil, beef, and sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, nasi goreng beef black pepper poses a moderate risk due to its combined saturated fat and high sodium load; choosing lean beef cuts, requesting less oil and sauce, and limiting serving frequency to occasional consumption is recommended to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Beef Black Pepper is a Malaysian and Singaporean stir-fried rice dish prepared by wok-frying pre-cooked white rice with sliced or minced beef, cracked black pepper, egg, garlic, onion, soy sauce, and oyster sauce over high heat. The dish is characterised by its bold, peppery, savoury flavour profile with a slightly smoky wok-hei aroma. The beef contributes a tender, meaty texture that contrasts with the firm, separated rice grains. Black pepper provides a sharp, pungent heat that distinguishes this variant from plain nasi goreng. It is commonly served as a single-plate meal in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops, and casual restaurants, often garnished with sliced spring onion, cucumber, and a fried egg on top. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 612 kcal, 29.8 g protein, 21.7 g fat, and 77 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content (~1680 mg) from the soy and oyster sauces. The low glycemic index (~54, at the upper end of the low GI category) and high glycemic load (~41.6 per serving) make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is generally halal-certified when prepared with halal beef and sauces. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertensive individuals, while the glycemic load is the primary concern for those with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for nasi goreng beef black pepper exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB nasi goreng (fried rice with egg) as the base, with protein and fat adjusted upward to account for beef content using USDA SR Legacy cooked beef data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo nasi goreng base and USDA SR Legacy cooked beef data. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or IMR source databases for this dish category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or IMR source databases for this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or IMR source databases for this dish category. Sodium value (~480 mg per 100 g) reflects typical hawker-style preparation with soy sauce and oyster sauce; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. The halal flag is set to true based on the typical preparation context at Malaysian and Singaporean halal-certified establishments; consumers should verify halal certification at specific restaurants. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fried rice, which falls into the 'low' GI category (55 or less); no direct measurement exists for this specific dish variant. Glycemic load per serving = (54 × 77.0) / 100 ≈ 41.6 based on 350 g serving size.
